#1 · record_id=4 · paper_id=1903.03825v3
Interpolation Consistency Training for Semi-Supervised Learning
Missing: 4raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

Table 2: Error rates (%) on SVHN using CNN-13 architecture. We ran three trials for ICT.

Gold Description

For SVHN, the test errors obtained by ICT are competitive with other state-of-the-art SSL methods (Table 2).

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which model achieves the lowest error rate for the 1000 labeled samples setting?",
    "rationale": "Identify the best-performing method when using 1000 labeled samples, which is the largest labeled set.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "How does the error rate of ICT compare to the supervised baseline across all labeled sizes?",
    "rationale": "Quantify the improvement of ICT over the basic supervised method to show the effectiveness of semi-supervised learning.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "For methods with complete entries (Supervised, Supervised (Mixup), Supervised (Manifold Mixup), Π model, TempEns, MT, SNTG, ICT), does increasing the number of labeled samples consistently reduce error rates?",
    "rationale": "Check the monotonic trend of error decreasing with more labeled data for each method.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "trend"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "Among the semi-supervised methods with 250 labeled samples, which one has the lowest error rate?",
    "rationale": "Identify the best semi-supervised method under the most data-scarce condition.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema",
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  }
]

#2 · record_id=18 · paper_id=2003.06729v1
NoiseRank: Unsupervised Label Noise Reduction with Dependence Models
Covered: 1Partially covered: 2raw 3verified 3source base

Caption

Table 3: Image classification on Food-101N results in terms of top-1 accuracy (%). Train data (310k) and test data (25k). CleanNet is trained with an additional 55k/5k (tr/va) verification labels to provide the required supervision on noise detection

Gold Description

In results table 3 and 4, in each row, the model is fine-tuned with the mentioned training examples on the specified pre-trained model (eg. "noisy train # 1" refers to the model # 1 referenced in the table that was trained using noisy training samples on ImageNet pre-trained Resnet-50). In Table 3 Food101-N, NoiseRank achieves state of the art 85.78% in top-1 accuracy compared to unsupervised 's 85.11%, and 11% error reduction over supervised noise reduction method CleanNet.

#3 · record_id=23 · paper_id=1907.06704v3
PPO Dash: Improving Generalization in Deep Reinforcement Learning
Partially covered: 1Missing: 2raw 3verified 3source retry

Caption

Table 5: Comparison of action sets and their impact on the maximum floor reached after 10m steps.

Gold Description

We found that the 8, 20, and 27 action sets all saw a similar improvement in performance going from an average of three floors to an average of five floors (see Table 5).

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which action set achieves the highest average floor after 10m steps?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the top-performing action set helps in selecting the best action configuration for maximizing floor reach.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "What is the performance difference between action sets with limit jump actions (✓) and the one without (Action Set 54)?",
    "rationale": "Comparing the average floor of sets with limit jump actions to that of the only set without reveals the impact of limiting jump actions.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "How many action sets achieve an average floor of 5.0?",
    "rationale": "Counting the number of action sets that reach the maximum observed floor identifies the prevalence of top performance.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "threshold_count"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "Is there a trend in the average floor as the action set number increases?",
    "rationale": "Examining the sequence of values may reveal whether larger action sets tend to perform better or worse.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "trend"
    ]
  }
]
Low-level questions
[

#4 · record_id=35 · paper_id=1909.12566v2
Distantly Supervised Question Parsing
Partially covered: 1Missing: 3raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

Table 2: Accuracies for relation linking task.

Gold Description

Table 2 summarizes the results in terms of accuracy. Despite the simplicity of the linking component in our approach, MDP-Parser achieves better results compared to the baselines, and again, improves the performance of EARL by almost 5% on all the datasets.

#5 · record_id=66 · paper_id=1909.11723v1
Revisit Knowledge Distillation: a Teacher-free framework
Partially covered: 1Missing: 3raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

Table 4: De-KD accuracy (in %) on two datasets. Pt-Teacher is “Poorly-trained Teacher”. Refer to the “Normal KD” in Tabs. 1 to 3 for the accuracy of students taught by “fully-trained teacher”.

Gold Description

From De-KD experiment results on CIFAR100 in Tab. 4, we observe that the student can be greatly promoted even when distilled by a poorly-trained teacher. From the De-KD experiment results on Tiny-ImageNet in Tab. 4, we find ResNet18 with 9.14% accuracy can still enhance the teacher model MobileNetV2 by 1.16%.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "What is the maximum De-KD accuracy across all table entries, and in which configuration does it occur?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the top performance helps understand the best-case scenario for De-KD.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "How does De-KD accuracy correlate with Pt-Teacher baseline accuracy across different runs?",
    "rationale": "Checking correlation can reveal if a better poorly-trained teacher leads to better student performance.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_correlation"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "When Student baseline is 2%, what is the range (min and max) of De-KD accuracies?",
    "rationale": "Since most rows have Student baseline 2%, this question isolates the effect of other variables.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "threshold_count",
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "Is there a clear difference in De-KD accuracy between the CIFAR100 and Tiny-ImageNet datasets?",
    "rationale": "Comparing datasets helps assess the generalizability of De-KD.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  }
]

#6 · record_id=91 · paper_id=1908.08704v1
Sequential Adversarial Learning for Self-Supervised Deep Visual Odometry
Partially covered: 4raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

Table 3: Ablation study on depth estimation for various versions of our method. Baseline denotes our framework without code, LSTM, discriminator (GAN) and trajectory consistency (TC) loss.

Gold Description

All the experiments are conducted on KITTI dataset and results are shown in Table 3, 4 In addition, adversarial learning gives the performance a further boost, and the temporal information actually improves depth.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which method achieves the best performance across all error metrics (Abs Rel, Sq Rel, RMSE, RMSE log) and accuracy thresholds (δ<1.25, δ<1.252, δ<1.253)?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the best performing model helps understand the overall contribution of each component in the ablation study.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_extrema",
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "How does the absolute relative error (Abs Rel) decrease from the baseline to each successive addition of components (code, GAN, LSTM, TC)?",
    "rationale": "Tracking the progression of Abs Rel shows the incremental benefit of each component on depth estimation accuracy.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison",
      "trend"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "What are the improvements in RMSE and RMSE log when comparing the baseline with the full model (Baseline+code+GAN+LSTM+TC)?",
    "rationale": "Comparing RMSE metrics quantifies the overall reduction in depth estimation error from the simplest to the most complex model.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "Is there a consistent increase in the accuracy thresholds (δ<1.25, δ<1.252, δ<1.253) as more components are added to the baseline?",
    "rationale": "Higher threshold values indicate better depth accuracy; tracking their changes reveals if improvements are consistent across all thresholds.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "trend",
      "threshold_count"
    ]
  }
]

#7 · record_id=92 · paper_id=1908.08704v1
Sequential Adversarial Learning for Self-Supervised Deep Visual Odometry
Partially covered: 1Missing: 2raw 3verified 3source retry

Caption

Table 4: Ablation study on pose estimation for various versions of our method on KITTI sequence 09 and 10. B denotes baseline.

Gold Description

As for pose estimation in Table 4, our baseline method performs much better than the other self-supervised VO approaches in literature (Table 2). This may mainly because of the joint use of depth and image for pose estimation (Eq. (5)). In addition, the accuracy is significantly improved by LSTM which incorporates historical information of multiple frames. The enforcement of trajectory consistency also brings about promising improvements in that it enforces geometric consistency among multiple pose estimations. Since depth is improved mainly on edges and details which takes up a small proportion, the accuracy gain is therefore limited. Yet the improved details are very important to RGBD matching for pose regression. Therefore, a slight increase in depth accuracy causes a big improvement in pose estimation.

Verified Candidate Insights + Matching

#1 The method B+code+GAN+LSTM+TC achieves the lowest pose estimation error on both KITTI Seq.09 (0.003) and Seq.10 (0.0029).
Partially coveredverifier: supportedconfidence: highanswers: q1, q2
Matched text: The enforcement of trajectory consistency also brings about promising improvements in that it enforces geometric consistency among multiple pose estimations. Since depth is improved mainly on edges and details which takes up a small proportion, the accuracy gain is therefore limited.
Matching reason: The gold description discusses improvements from LSTM and trajectory consistency, implying the full model (with both) is better, but does not explicitly state it achieves the lowest error or provide the specific values.
Verifier reason: Answers q1 and q2 directly state that B+code+GAN+LSTM+TC has the minimum values (0.003 and 0.0029) for Seq.09 and Seq.10, exactly matching the claim.

#8 · record_id=96 · paper_id=1903.07902v6
A Comparative Study for Unsupervised Network Representation Learning
Missing: 2raw 2verified 2source retry

Caption

TABLE VI: Multilabel Node Classification results in terms of Micro-F1 and Macro-F1. All results are mean of 5-fold cross validations. ✗ indicates the corresponding method failed to finish for the given dataset. ’n.a’ indicates the given method is ’not applicable’ to the corresponding graph.

Gold Description

We report the Micro-F1 and Macro-F1 scores after a 5-fold multi-label classification using one-vs-rest logistic regression. In this section, we look at the results from node classification (Table 6).

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which method achieves the highest Micro-F1 on the Reddit dataset and what is that value?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the top-performing method on a specific dataset helps determine which algorithm is most effective for that graph type.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "Among methods with complete results (no nulls), which shows the largest average performance drop from Micro-F1 to Macro-F1 across all datasets?",
    "rationale": "A large drop may indicate that the method is biased toward majority classes, which is important for understanding classification robustness.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "How many methods achieve a Micro-F1 above 70 on the PubMed dataset, and which ones are they?",
    "rationale": "Counting methods that exceed a threshold provides insight into which methods perform well on biomedical citation networks.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "threshold_count"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "Does GraphSAGE-GCN consistently outperform GraphSAGE across all datasets in terms of both Micro-F1 and Macro-F1?",
    "rationale": "Comparing two similar methods across multiple metrics reveals whether the GCN variant provides universal improvements.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  }
]

#9 · record_id=97 · paper_id=1802.01096v2
Software Engineers vs.Machine Learning Algorithms: An Empirical Study Assessing Performance and Reuse Tasks
Missing: 4raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

TABLE III: Correlation between participants expertises in the Internet of Things with their solution results.

Gold Description

After executing the solution proposed by each participant, we connect that solution's results with the participant's knowledge in the IoT domain, as shown in Table 3. Thus, we performed statistical analyses, as described by Peck and Devore , of the measures presented in Table 3.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which participant achieved the highest solution performance (fitness average) and what is that value?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the maximum value helps understand the best performance among participants, which is a typical extreme analysis.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "Which participant achieved the lowest solution performance and what is that value?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the minimum value highlights the weakest performance, useful for performance gap analysis.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "How many participants scored above the overall average of 55.48?",
    "rationale": "Counting participants above the average threshold gives insight into the distribution of performance relative to the mean.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "threshold_count"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "How does the performance of the 'Learning' participant compare to the 'zeroed' participant?",
    "rationale": "Comparing these two special participants reveals whether prior learning or lack thereof correlates with performance.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  }
]

#10 · record_id=98 · paper_id=1802.01096v2
Software Engineers vs.Machine Learning Algorithms: An Empirical Study Assessing Performance and Reuse Tasks
Covered: 1Partially covered: 1Missing: 2raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

TABLE IV: Data to perform test statistic.

Gold Description

As shown in Table 4, we separated the results of the experiments into two groups: i) software engineers with IoT knowledge and ii) software engineers without IoT knowledge. Then, we calculated the mean and the standard deviation of Accordingly, for a distribution with 7 degrees of freedom (see Table 4) and a confidence level of 99%, the negative tcriticalvalue is -3.00. As shown in Table 4, the performance mean (x) of the IoT expert software engineers' solutions is 52.46 and the standard deviation (σ) is 10.91. As shown in Table 4, the performance mean (x) of the solutions from software engineers without experience in IoT development is 32.61 and the standard deviation (σ) is 15.15. As shown in Table 4, this T-distribution has 5 degrees of freedom.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which group has the highest mean and median performance, and what is the gap between the best and worst performing groups?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the top performer and the performance gap helps to benchmark against others.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema",
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "How does the standard deviation of Software Engineers without IoT knowledge compare to those with IoT knowledge, and what does this imply about performance consistency?",
    "rationale": "Comparing variability reveals which group is more consistent in performance.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "What is the difference in mean between Software Engineers with IoT knowledge and the Machine-learning based approach, noting that the ML approach has only one sample?",

      "row_comparison",
      "threshold_count"

  },

    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema",
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  }
]

      "Software Engineers without IoT knowledge"
    ],
    "column": "n samples",
    "threshold": "1"
  }
]

Caption

TABLE III: Performance on Yale Faces data sets.

Gold Description

From the results shown in Table III, we see that the percentage of data needed goes up as K increases. The performance results are shown in the last section of Table III.

#12 · record_id=119 · paper_id=1905.13613v1
Subspace Networks for Few-shot Classification
Partially covered: 3Missing: 1raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

Table 1: Identical domain and domain shift accuracies for 5-way 5-shot classification using a ResNet-10 backbone and mini-ImageNet and CUB datasets. The best-performing method is highlighted.

Gold Description

(See Table 1 for detailed statistics and 95% confidence intervals.) Table 1 and Figure 3 present our results in the context of existing state-of-the-art distance-metric learning based methods. The intra-domain difference between classes is higher in mini-ImageNet than in CUB (Chen et al. ), which is reflected by a drop of the test accuracies of all methods. It can also be seen that when the domain difference between the training and test stage classes increases (left to right in Figure 3) the performance of all few-shot algorithms lowers. As expected, the classification accuracy lowers slightly when going from the mini-ImageNet → CUB to the CUB → mini-ImageNet setting. Interestingly though, it does not decrease substantially. This shows that the different feature embeddings generalize well, at least across these two different datasets. Compared to other distance-metric learning based methods, subspace networks show significantly better performance in both the mini-ImageNet → CUB setting and CUB → mini-ImageNet setting.

Matched text: Compared to other distance-metric learning based methods, subspace networks show significantly better performance in both the mini-ImageNet → CUB setting and CUB → mini-ImageNet setting.
Matching reason: The gold description only mentions subspace networks' superior performance in the two domain shift settings, not in the identical domain settings (CUB and mini-ImageNet). Thus, the claim is partially covered.
Verifier reason: Each supporting answer confirms SubspaceNet has the maximum value in the corresponding column.

#4 There is a positive correlation of 0.93 between accuracy on CUB and accuracy on mini-ImageNet CUB across methods.
Missingverifier: rewriteconfidence: highanswers: q11
Matching reason: The gold description does not mention any correlation between accuracies on CUB and mini-ImageNet CUB. It discusses performance drops and domain differences but no correlation coefficient.
Verifier reason: The original claim added the interpretive word 'strong' and rounded the correlation to 0.93, but the answer provides a correlation of 0.9328 and states 'positive correlation'. The rewritten claim avoids interpretation and remains factual.

#13 · record_id=126 · paper_id=1911.09837v3
Graph Convolution Networks for Probabilistic Modeling of Driving Acceleration
Missing: 4raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

TABLE III: Jerk Sign Inversions Per Trajectory

Gold Description

Table III shows the number of negative headway occurrences over number of simulated trajectories for all models. Consistent with RMSE analysis, the results from Table III demonstrates that original GCN models often produce poor acceleration predictions which lead to unrealistic states.

#14 · record_id=137 · paper_id=1907.05282v1

Partially covered: 1raw 1verified 1source retry

Caption

Table 2: Evaluation of SA with different growth rates on Set14 with 4× up-scaling factor.

Gold Description

As shown in Table 2, SA improves PSNR more than 0.1db in each growth rate, and it increases output RCIR with a large extent, thus high-frequency details of an image tend to be recovered more clearly. Besides, compared SA (G = 20) with noSA (G = 32), they have the almost same parameters (1.52M and 1.54M), but SA still achieves higher RCIR and SSIM than no SA. Notably, increasing G

#1 The SA-32 configuration achieves the highest PSNR (28.55) and SSIM (0.7848) among all configurations on Set14 with 4× up-scaling.
Partially coveredverifier: supportedconfidence: highanswers: q1, q2
Matched text: SA improves PSNR more than 0.1db in each growth rate
Matching reason: The gold description confirms that SA improves PSNR and SSIM (via benefits to RCIR and recovery of high-frequency details), but does not explicitly state that SA-32 is the highest among all configurations. The claim adds a ranking not directly present in the description.
Verifier reason: The claim is directly supported by both executor answers: q1 confirms SA-32 has maximum PSNR 28.55, q2 confirms SA-32 has maximum SSIM 0.7848. No additional facts or interpretations are added.

#15 · record_id=143 · paper_id=1804.03166v5
Improving Confidence Estimates for Unfamiliar Examples
Partially covered: 1Missing: 2raw 4verified 3source retry

Caption

Table 2: T-scaling calibration effectively reduces likelihood error (NLL, Brier) and calibration error (ECE) for many models across tasks for familiar and unfamiliar samples. Without calibration, using an ensemble reduces these errors, but an ensemble of calibrated models (“Ens. T-scale”) performs best. Applying T-scaling to an ensemble of uncalibrated classifiers, and creating an ensemble of calibrated classifiers produces nearly identical results.

Gold Description

els outperform uncalibrated models, and ensembles of calibrated models outperform ensembles of uncalibrated models. For example, in cat vs. dog recognition, the baseline NLL drops from 0.423 to 0.295, a 30% reduction; and the ensemble NLL drops from 0.286 to 0.229, a 20% reduction. Table 2 compares performance of the baseline and ensemble methods, both without and with T-scale calibration. Calibrated single mod For the object presence task, there is little effect of calibration We also found calibration to improve the Bayesian method . Calibration has little effect for distillation and G-distillation, likely because distillation's fitting to soft labels makes it less confident.

]

Caption

Table 2: Validation of our auto-annotation for video instance segmentation on KITTI MOTS validation set (car class).

Gold Description

Table 2 shows the results on the KITTI Eigen split test, where the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance in the single view depth prediction task with unsupervised training.

#17 · record_id=167 · paper_id=1902.06684v1
Learning Topological Representation for Networks via Hierarchical Sampling Identify applicable funding agency here. If none, delete this.
Covered: 2Contradicted: 2raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

TABLE II: AUC of link prediction.

Gold Description

Each experiment is independently implemented for 20 times and the average performances on the testing set are reported in Table II. HSRL significantly outperforms all baselines on all datasets. For the small and sparse network movielens, the improvements of HSRL(DW), HSRL(N2V), and HSRL(LINE) are 3.6%, 2.5%, and 16.6% respectively. For the dense network MIT, HSRL(DW), HSRL(N2V), and HSRL(LINE) outperform the baselines by 2.9%, 8.5%, and 0.5%. For three large networks, DBLP, Yelp, and Douban, the improvement of HSRL is striking: the improvements of HSRL(DW), HSRL(N2V), and HSRL(LINE) are 6.3%, 19.9%, 3.5% for DBLP, 6.5%, 16.8%, 5.9% for Yelp, and 18.4%, 30.5%, 17.5% for Douban. The results of HARP on movielens, DBLP, Yelp, and Douban are worse than the original NRL methods. Moreover, the performance of HARP(LINE) is drastically worse than LINE. It only works better than DeepWalk, node2vec on MIT which is a small and dense network. The compressed networks generated by HARP on a network could not reveal its global topologies. Hence, using node embeddings of compressed networks as initialization could mislead the NRL methods to a bad local minimum. Such an issue could occur especially when the input network is large-scale and the objective function of the NRL method is highly non-convex, e.g., LINE. The improvements of HSRL on DBLP, Yelp, and Duban are larger than that on Movielens and MIT. It demonstrates that HSRL works much better than baselines on large-scale networks.

#18 · record_id=171 · paper_id=1911.13044v3
Learning wih Modular Representations for Long-Term Multi-Agent Motion Predictions
Missing: 4raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

Table 1: Reported results assume observation length of Tobs=1.6sec and prediction lengths of Tpred=3.2sec and Tpred=4.8sec. First 4 rows correspond to the Average Displacement Error and last 4 to the final displacement error. Each result represents an average obtained from 5 independent runs with different random seeds.

Gold Description

The proposed solution outperforms the alternatives. Table 1 shows the results obtained on this task. it can be observed that the variability of the predicted trajectories is affected by the uncertainty of the global knowledge of the environment. a well optimised vanilla LSTM with stochastic output often outperforms alternative approaches. we observed results proportional to the quality of the learned global model of the environment. Building an environmental model for the UCY Zara01 and Zara02 datasets is particularly challenging and thus the results are not substantially better than alternatives. importance of environmental or spatial information when making predictions.

  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "For Tpred=3.2s, which dataset has the lowest average displacement error (ADE)?",

      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },

      "row_comparison"
    ]

#19 · record_id=176 · paper_id=1805.01128v2
Local Critic Training of Deep Neural Networks
Partially covered: 3raw 3verified 3source retry

Caption

TABLE III: Average test accuracy (%) with respect to the update frequency of local critic networks.

Gold Description

we compare different update frequency in Table III. When the update frequency is a half of that for the main network (i.e., 1/2), the accuracy drops by 0.48% and 1.92% for the two datasets, respectively. Then, the decrease of the accuracy is only 0.56% for CIFAR-10 and 1.60% for CIFAR100 when the update frequency decreases from 1/2 to 1/5.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "What is the maximum average test accuracy achieved on each dataset and at which update frequency?",
    "rationale": "This identifies the best performance and the corresponding update frequency.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },

    "id": "h2",
    "question": "How does the average test accuracy change with decreasing update frequency (from 1/1 to 1/5) for CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100?",
    "rationale": "This examines the monotonicity and pattern of accuracy as update frequency decreases.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "trend"
    ]
  },

    "id": "h3",
    "question": "Which dataset experiences a larger accuracy drop from frequency 1/1 to 1/5, and what is the magnitude of the drop?",

    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]

    "suggested_operations": [
      "threshold_count"
    ]
  }
]

#20 · record_id=178 · paper_id=1805.01128v2
Local Critic Training of Deep Neural Networks
Covered: 3Partially covered: 1raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

TABLE VI: Average FLOPs and accuracy of progressive inference for test data of CIFAR-10 when the threshold is set to 0.9 or 0.95.

Gold Description

The results are shown in Table VI. When the threshold is 0.9, with only a slight loss of accuracy (92.39% to 91.18%), the computational complexity is reduced significantly, which is only 18.45% of that of the main model. When the threshold increases to 0.95, the accuracy loss becomes smaller (only 0.64%), while the complexity reduction remains almost the same (19.40% of the main model's complexity).

Mask Combination of Multi-layer Graphs for Global Structure Inference
Covered: 2Partially covered: 1Missing: 2raw 5verified 5source retry

Caption

TABLE I: Global Graph Recovery and Mask Recovery Performances

Gold Description

The findings are summarized in Table I. GL-conv yields a high difference between the recall and the precision rate which leads to poor F-score compared to other methods. The MSE score of ML and GL-conv is better than the one of GL-informed. ML achieves good rates on the

Table 1: Rerank evaluation on real-world dataset with random splitting.

Gold Description

IRecGAN achieved encouraging P@10 improvement against all baselines. PGIS did not perform as well as PG PGU was able to fit the given data more accurately than PG PGU performed worse than IRecGAN LSTMD outperformed LSTM,

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which model yields the highest P@10 and which yields the lowest P@10?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the top and bottom performers in the P@10 metric helps in understanding the relative effectiveness of different models for this precision measure.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "Which model yields the highest P@1 and which yields the lowest P@1?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the top and bottom performers in the P@1 metric highlights which models are best at making accurate top-1 recommendations.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "For each model, what is the difference between its P@10 and P@1? Which model has the largest absolute difference?",
    "rationale": "The difference between P@10 and P@1 may indicate consistency in ranking performance across different cutoff levels; a large gap could suggest a model is better at broader ranking than at the very top.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison",
      "row_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "How many models have a P@10 exceeding 34%?",
    "rationale": "A threshold count of P@10 above 34% gives an indication of how many models achieve a relatively high precision at the top 10 positions, which may be a practical performance benchmark.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "threshold_count"
    ]
  }
]

#23 · record_id=188 · paper_id=1905.10705v1
Modeling treatment events in disease progression
Partially covered: 2Missing: 1raw 3verified 3source retry

Caption

Figure 2: Test error on GDI dataset

Gold Description

We run all five methods on the same training/validation/test set for 40 times and compare the mean and sd of test-errors. The results are presented in Table 2 and Figure 2. Compared with the null model pMean (Column 2 of Table 2), fPCA gives roughly the same order of error; CSI, SLI and rowMean provide better predictions, achieving 62%, 66% and 73% of the test errors respectively. In particular, our algorithm CSI improves the result of vanilla model SLI by 7%, it also provide a stable estimation with the smallest sd across multiple selections of test sets.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which method achieves the smallest mean test error?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the best performing method in terms of average error.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },

    "id": "h2",
    "question": "Which method has the largest standard deviation?",
    "rationale": "Assessing variability of test errors across methods.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },

    "id": "h3",
    "question": "How many methods have a scaled mean greater than 1?",

    ]

    "question": "Is there a positive correlation between mean test error and standard deviation?",

Matched text: our algorithm CSI improves the result of vanilla model SLI by 7%

#3 fPCA has the highest standard deviation (13.54) and is the only method with a scaled mean greater than 1.
Partially coveredverifier: rewriteconfidence: highanswers: q2, q3
Matched text: fPCA gives roughly the same order of error
Matching reason: Gold description confirms fPCA's error is similar to the null model, implying scaled mean close to 1, but does not state it is greater than 1. The claim about highest standard deviation is not mentioned in gold description.
Verifier reason: The original claim adds an interpretation ('indicating it performs worse than the baseline') not present in the executor answers. The rewritten claim uses only the facts from q2 and q3.

#24 · record_id=204 · paper_id=1903.11027v5
nuScenes: A multimodal dataset for autonomous driving
Partially covered: 3Missing: 1raw 5verified 4source retry

Caption

Table 3: PointPillars [51] detection performance on the val set. We can see that more lidar sweeps lead to a significant performance increase and that pretraining with ImageNet is on par with KITTI.

Gold Description

The results show that both detection and velocity estimates improve with an increasing number of lidar sweeps but with diminishing rate of return (Table 3). the final performance of the network only marginally varied between different pretrainings (Table 3).

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "How does the number of lidar sweeps affect NDS and mAP?",
    "rationale": "The caption states that more sweeps increase performance. This question examines that trend across 1, 5, and 10 sweeps.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "trend"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "Which lidar sweep configuration achieves the lowest mAVE (best velocity estimation)?",
    "rationale": "mAVE measures velocity error; lower is better. Identifying the best configuration provides insight into optimal settings.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "Among configurations with 10 lidar sweeps, how consistent are the performance metrics (NDS, mAP, mAVE)?",
    "rationale": "The table includes three entries for 10 sweeps. This question assesses variability across runs with the same sweep count.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "Do NDS and mAP have a positive correlation across the different lidar sweep configurations?",
    "rationale": "Both metrics measure detection quality; checking correlation helps understand if they improve together.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_correlation"
    ]
  }
]

#25 · record_id=257 · paper_id=2002.01113v1
Efficient Riemannian Optimization on the Stiefel Manifold via the Cayley Transform
Missing: 3raw 4verified 3source retry

Caption

Table 4: Pixel-by-pixel MNIST accuracy and training time per iteration of the closed-form Cayley Transform, Cayley SGD, and Cayley ADAM for Full-uRNNs (Wisdom et al., 2016). All experiments are performed on one TITAN Xp GPU.

Gold Description

Wisdom et al. (2016) restricted the transition unitary matrix on the Stiefel manifold via a closed-form Cayley transform. On the contrary, we use Cayley SGD with momentum and Cayley ADAM to reduce the training time. Table 4 shows that the proposed algorithms reduce the training time by about 35% for all settings of the network, while maintaining the same level of accuracy. All experiments are performed on one TITAN Xp GPU.

#26 · record_id=263 · paper_id=1809.08410v1
Entropy-Assisted Multi-Modal Emotion Recognition Framework Based on Physiological Signals
Partially covered: 4Missing: 1raw 5verified 5source retry

Caption

TABLE I: P-value of RCMSE features of ECG signals (Left:Arousal, Right:Valence)

Gold Description

The p-value of RCMSE and MMSE of ECG and GSR are shown in Table and II. RCMSE of ECG has the best p-value when setting scaling factor to 2 for both arousal and valence.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which combination of m and scale yields the minimum p-value?",
    "rationale": "To identify the most statistically significant feature in the RCMSE analysis.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema",
      "row_extrema"
    ]
  },

#27 · record_id=273 · paper_id=2003.03021v1
Exploiting Verified Neural Networks via Floating Point Numerical Error
Missing: 4raw 4verified 4source base

Caption

Table 1: Number of Successful Adversarial Attacks for Different Implementations

Gold Description

We apply random perturbations outlined in Algorithm 1 to these quasi-adversarial inputs (x1 from Section 3.2 Step 2) to obtain adversarial inputs for the verified robust input image (x0 = αxseed from Section 3.2 Step 1). All the implementations that we have considered are successfully attacked. We present the detailed numbers in Table 1.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which implementation achieves the highest number of successful adversarial attacks on MNIST, and which on CIFAR10?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the most effective implementation per dataset highlights performance gaps across mitigation strategies.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "How does the number of quasi-adversarial examples tested relate to the total successful attacks across all implementations for each dataset?",
    "rationale": "A strong correlation could indicate that more tested quasi-adversarial examples lead to more discovered attacks, affecting interpretation of results.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_correlation"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "For CIFAR10, which implementation results in the fewest successful attacks, suggesting better robustness?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the weakest or strongest defense helps prioritize verification methods.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison",
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "Is there a consistent ranking of implementation effectiveness across the two datasets?",
    "rationale": "Consistency would suggest generalizable trends; divergence would indicate dataset-specific effects.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison",
      "trend"
    ]
  }
]

#28 · record_id=276 · paper_id=2003.05747v1
Fast local linear regression with anchor regularization
Partially covered: 1Missing: 2raw 3verified 3source retry

Caption

Table 1: Averaged mean squared error on the real-world datasets over 50 runs. For each method, we select the best parameters by using 3-fold cross-validation. N/A means that a memory problem occurred when computing the model on the dataset. The asterisk * means that the results were computed without cross-validation but with fixed parameters (because of a too long runtime).

Gold Description

Averaged mean squared error on the real-world datasets over 50 runs. For each method, we select the best parameters by using 3-fold cross-validation. N/A means that a memory problem occurred when computing the model on the dataset. The asterisk * means that the results were computed without cross-validation but with fixed parameters (because of a too long runtime). We compared our method on several datasets with other methods. We computed the average mean squared error with 50 runs and the training and test time. We compared the proposed FALL model with KNN, kernel ridge regression (KRR), ridge and Lasso , and network Lasso . It should be noted that network Lasso is a state-of-the-art locally linear model. To compare the methods fairly, we selected the best hyperparameters by cross-validation with optuna with a timeout of 60 s. Through experiments, the proposed FALL method was found to compare favorably with network Lasso and other existing baselines. In particular, the concrete data, the FALL method significantly outperforms baseline methods. Overall, because the FALL method is a locally linear model, the performance of the FALL method is similar to the one with KNN.

#29 · record_id=277 · paper_id=2003.05747v1
Fast local linear regression with anchor regularization
Missing: 4raw 4verified 4source base

Caption

Table 2: Training time and testing time (in seconds) on real-world datasets over 50 runs. For FALL, the training time is the sum of the creation of the anchor models and the final model. N/A means that a memory problem occurred when computing the model on the dataset. The asterisk * means that the training time and testing time were averaged with few samples (because of a too long runtime).

Gold Description

Training time and testing time (in seconds) on real-world datasets over 50 runs. For FALL, the training time is the sum of the creation of the anchor models and the final model. N/A means that a memory problem occurred when computing the model on the dataset. The asterisk * means that the training time and testing time were averaged with few samples (because of a too long runtime). The important point here is that the computation time of the FALL method is two orders of magnitude smaller than that of network Lasso.

High-level questions

    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which method achieves the shortest training time across all datasets?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the fastest training method helps in selecting computationally efficient models.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema",
      "row_comparison"

#30 · record_id=284 · paper_id=2002.02618v1
Finding Quantum Critical Points with Neural-Network Quantum States
Missing: 1raw 1verified 1source base

Caption

Table 2: The performance evaluation of RBM-NQS-CS and RBM-NQS-I for one-dimensional system in Ising model where the system size is 128 and parameter of the system J/|h|=3.0. The reported value is average value over 20 realisations. The value inside the parentheses is the standard deviation.

Gold Description

In the case of a deep ferromagnetic phase (J/|h| = 3.0 in Table 2) and antiferromagnetic phase (J/|h| = −3.0 in Table 3), we observe that the results of the RBM-NQS-I are closer to the result of the tensor network method and need less iterations to converge to the stopping criterion than RBM-NQS-CS.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which method achieves the lowest (most negative) energy among all columns?",
    "rationale": "Energy is a key performance metric; the lowest value indicates the best approximation to the ground state.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "How do the trained RBM-NQS methods compare to the Tensor network baseline in terms of M2_F and M2_A?",
    "rationale": "M2_F and M2_A are measures of fidelity; comparing these helps assess accuracy relative to the exact reference.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "Is there a correlation between the number of training iterations and the final energy for the trained methods?",
    "rationale": "Only trained methods have iteration counts; exploring correlation may indicate convergence behavior.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_correlation"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "Across all metrics (excluding Iterations), which row shows the largest absolute difference between RBM-NQS-CS and RBM-NQS-I after training?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the metric with the largest performance gap highlights where one method significantly outperforms the other.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_extrema"
    ]
  }
]

#31 · record_id=307 · paper_id=1910.08635v1
Tree-based Intelligent Intrusion Detection System in Internet of Vehicles
Partially covered: 1Missing: 3raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

TABLE V: Top-3 Feature Importance by Each Attack

Gold Description

The list of top-3 important features and their corresponding weights of each attack is shown in Table V. As Table V shows, the destination port can reflect a DoS, brute force, web attack, and botnet attack. The size of packets is another important feature. For example, the average packet size indicates a DoS attack, port scan attack, and web attack. The packet length in the forward direction is related to port scan, brute-force, and infiltration attacks, while the packet length in the backward direction reflects a DoS, web attack, and botnet. In addition, the variance of the length of the packets in both forward and backward directions reflects the brute force attack, and the count of pushing flags indicates port scan attack. After getting the feature importance list of each attack, the IDSs with other aims like designing a dedicated system for detecting a single type of attack can be developed by selecting the important features based on the list.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which feature has the highest weight across all attack types?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the globally most important feature can help prioritize network monitoring and feature engineering efforts.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "For each attack type, how does the weight of the top feature compare to the second and third features?",
    "rationale": "This reveals the dominance of the primary feature and whether the drop-off is steep, indicating a clear leading indicator.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "How many distinct features appear in the top-3 lists across all attacks?",
    "rationale": "A small number of repeated features suggests common indicators, while many unique features indicate attack-specific patterns.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "threshold_count"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "Is there a correlation between the weight of 'Destination Port' (or its variants) and the attack type?",
    "rationale": "Destination Port appears in multiple attacks with varying weights; analyzing its importance per attack can reveal if it is a general or attack-specific predictor.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_correlation"
    ]
  }
]

#32 · record_id=309 · paper_id=1905.02780v1
Uncertainty-Aware Data Aggregation for Deep Imitation Learning
Missing: 3raw 3verified 3source base

Caption

TABLE II: Performance Comparison on Intersections Benchmark. Avg success rate and distance traveled between infractions are reported. Distance traveled between infractions can be higher for a model with lower success rate due to its failure in learning turning behaviors.

Gold Description

The performance8 of the models trained with different datasets is shown in Table II. The model trained with the active filter dataset outperformed that with baseline as we hypothesized. the model trained with active filter also achieved better performance than the model using all the passive data,

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which dataset achieves the highest success rate in Town1 and Town2 respectively?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the best-performing method in each town highlights the most effective approach for that specific environment.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "Is there a positive correlation between success rate and km per infraction across datasets?",
    "rationale": "Understanding if higher success rates correspond to longer distances between infractions helps evaluate trade-offs.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_correlation"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "How does the infraction rate of UAIL compare to other methods that report this metric?",
    "rationale": "Comparing infraction rates among methods that report them reveals relative safety performance.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "Which dataset shows the largest gap between success rates in Town1 and Town2?",
    "rationale": "A large gap may indicate overfitting to one town or difficulty in generalization.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  }
]

#33 · record_id=323 · paper_id=1909.05125v1
Byzantine-Robust Federated Learning through Adaptive Model Averaging
Partially covered: 2Missing: 1raw 5verified 3source base

Caption

Table 1: Averaged test error over 10 random training data splits for AFA, FA, MKRUM and COMED in different scenarios: clean datasets for all clients, Byzantine adversaries, label flipping attacks, and noisy clients. For each scenario we considered two settings with 10 and 100 clients with 3 and 30 bad/malicious clients respectively. Best results with statistical significance at the 5% level, according to a Wilcoxon Rank-Sum test, are highlighted in bold.

Gold Description

Thus, for each experiment in Table 1 we highlight those results where one or two algorithms perform significantly better than the others (according to the outcome of the statistical test). From the results we can observe that AFA is robust in all scenarios for the 4 datasets, i.e. the test error of the algorithm is not significantly degraded compared to the performance achieved in the clean scenarios. As expected, FA’s performance is significantly affected under thepresence of bad clients, especially in byzantine and flipping scenarios. It is also interesting to observe that both MKRUM and COMED are very sensitive to label flipping attacks, especially in MNIST, FMNIST and CIFAR-10 for the case of 10 clients. For example, in MNIST, MKRUM and COMED test errors increases up to 78.98% and 11.55% respectively in the flipping attack scenario with 10 clients, whereas AFA’s error is just 2.96%, similar to its performance in the clean scenario. MKRUM also offers a worse performance in the clean scenario compared to the other algorithms, which is especially noticeable in the case of CIFAR-10, where the error rate is 53.02%, i.e. a 20% more than FA’s error. This is not the case for COMED, whose performance is similar toAFA and FA when no bad clients are present, except for FM-NIST, where COMED exhibits a better performance. How- ever, COMED is not consistently robust to all the scenariosconsidered in the experiment.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which method achieves the lowest test error (best performance) in each scenario and client size?",
    "rationale": "Identify the overall best-performing method across all settings by finding the minimum test error per column.",

      "column_extrema",
      "row_comparison"
    ]

    "id": "h2",

      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },

      "row_comparison",
      "threshold_count"
    ]
  }
]

#34 · record_id=336 · paper_id=1912.07819v1
Angular Learning: Toward Discriminative Embedded Features
Covered: 1Missing: 3raw 4verified 4source base

Caption

Table 6: Recognition error rate (mean±std%) on CIFAR10 without data augmentation and CIFAR10+ with data augmentation. Every result is evaluated five times.

Gold Description

Table 6 and 7 reveal Arc is the best and ArcFace is better.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "On CIFAR10+ (with data augmentation), which method achieves the lowest recognition error rate?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the best performer on the augmented dataset helps understand which method benefits most from augmentation.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "Among methods with the same parameter count (11.22M: Softmax, Center, Arc, ArcFace), how does error rate vary on CIFAR10+?",
    "rationale": "Comparing methods with identical model size reveals the impact of the loss function on performance.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "Does the All-CNN method, with only 1.3M parameters, achieve a lower error rate on CIFAR10 than methods with 11.22M parameters?",
    "rationale": "Assessing parameter efficiency: All-CNN uses fewer parameters but may still perform competitively.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "How many methods have an error rate below 10% on CIFAR10 (without augmentation)?",
    "rationale": "Counting methods that exceed a reasonable performance threshold gives a sense of overall quality.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "threshold_count"
    ]
  }
]

#35 · record_id=341 · paper_id=2001.04643v1
DDSP: Differentiable Digital Signal Processing
Partially covered: 1Missing: 3raw 4verified 4source base

Caption

Table 1: Resynthesis accuracies. Comparison of DDSP models to SOTA WaveRNN model provided the same conditioning information. The supervised DDSP Autoencoder and WaveRNN models use the fundamental frequency from a pretrained CREPE model, while the unsupervised DDSP autoencoder learns to infer the frequency from the audio during training.

Gold Description

Table 1: Resynthesis accuracies. Comparison of DDSP models to SOTA WaveRNN model provided the same conditioning information. The supervised DDSP Autoencoder and WaveRNN models use the fundamental frequency from a pretrained CREPE model, while the unsupervised DDSP autoencoder learns to infer the frequency from the audio during training. For the NSynth dataset, we quantitatively compare the quality of DDSP resynthesis with that of a state-of-the-art baseline using WaveRNN (Hantrakul et al., 2019). The models are comparable as they are trained on the same data, provided the same conditioning, and both targeted towards realtime synthesis applications. In Table 1, we compute loudness and fundamental frequency (F0) L1 metrics described in Section of the appendix. Despite the strong performance of the baseline, the supervised DDSP autoencoder still outperforms it, especially in F0 L1. This is not unexpected, as the additive synthesizer directly uses the conditioning frequency to synthesize audio. The unsupervised DDSP autoencoder must learn to infer its own F0 conditioning signal directly from the audio. As described in Section B.4, we improve optimization by also adding a perceptual loss in the form of a pretrained CREPE network (Kim et al., 2018). While not as accurate as the supervised DDSP version, the model does a fair job at learning to generate sounds with the correct frequencies without supervision, outperforming the supervised WaveRNN model.

#36 · record_id=343 · paper_id=1812.10366v2
A Poisson-Gaussian Denoising Dataset with Real Fluorescence Microscopy Images
Covered: 1Missing: 3raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

Table 2: Denoising performance using the mixed test set, which includes confocal, two-photon, and wide-field microscopy images. PSNR (dB), SSIM, and denoising time (seconds) are obtained by averaging over 48 noise realizations in the mixed test set for each of 5 noise levels. Results of DnCNN and Noise2Noise are obtained by training on dataset with all noise levels. All 50 captures of each FOV (except the 19-th FOV which is reserved for test) are included in the training set, with 1 (DnCNN) or 2 (Noise2Noise) samples of which randomly selected from each FOV when forming mini-batches during training for 400 epochs. †Note that test time for deep learning models on GPU is faster in orders of magnitude, i.e. 0.62 ms for DnCNN and 0.99 ms for Noise2Noise on single GPU in our experiment.

Gold Description

The benchmark denoising results on the mixed test set is shown in Table 2, including PSNR, structural similarity index (SSIM) and denoising time. From the table, BM3D (in combination with VST) is still the most versatile traditional denoising algorithm regarding its high PSNR and relatively fast denoising speed. PURE-LET, though its PSNR is not the highest, is the fastest denoising method among all the benchmarked algorithms thanks to its specific design for Poisson-Gaussian denoising. Finally, deep learning models outperform the other 8 methods by a significant margin in all noise levels, both in terms of PSNR and SSIM, even thought they are blind to noise levels. This is different from the observation made before in [1, 24], probably because the nature of Poisson dominated noise is different from Gaussian noise while most of the denoising methods are developed for Gaussian noise model. Even if we applied the VST before Gaussian denoising, the transformed noise may still be different from a pure Gaussian one. More importantly, here the models are re-trained with our FMD dataset instead of pre-trained on other datasets.

#2 PURE-LET has the shortest denoising time among all methods, at 2.61 seconds.
Coveredverifier: supportedconfidence: highanswers: q9
Matched text: PURE-LET, though its PSNR is not the highest, is the fastest denoising method among all the benchmarked algorithms thanks to its specific design for Poisson-Gaussian denoising.
Matching reason: The gold description explicitly states that PURE-LET is the fastest method, matching the claim.
Verifier reason: Executor answer q9 confirms that PURE-LET has the minimum time value of 2.61 seconds, supporting the claim directly.

#37 · record_id=350 · paper_id=1907.00749v1
Deep Multi-Task Learning for Anomalous Driving Detection Using CAN Bus Scalar Sensor Data
Covered: 1Partially covered: 1Missing: 2raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

TABLE II: Comparison of normalized reconstruction MSE losses.

Gold Description

Table II shows the average normalized reconstruction loss on test data for different modalities between our approach (multitask learning) and LSTM autoencoder. We can notice that, our approach results in lower reconstruction loss with 33% lower error (0.2) compared to the standalone autoencoder (0.3) in the 'combined' category.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which feature has the highest reconstruction loss for each model?",
    "rationale": "Identify the feature most difficult to reconstruct for LSTM Autoencoder and Our Approach, revealing potential weaknesses.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema",
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },

    "id": "h2",
    "question": "For which features does Our Approach achieve lower loss than LSTM Autoencoder?",
    "rationale": "Highlight features where Our Approach improves reconstruction, indicating areas of model superiority.",

      "row_comparison",
      "threshold_count"
    ]
  },

    "id": "h3",

      "row_comparison",
      "row_extrema"
    ]
  },

      "row_comparison",
      "trend"
    ]
  }
]

#38 · record_id=370 · paper_id=2002.10981v1
Covered: 2Missing: 2raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

Table IV: Loss and Accuracy Calculation Result

Gold Description

We calculate the average log loss and accuracy during training and testing of our models and display the results in Table IV. For both models, lower training losses are calculated as compared to the test case. Here, Model 1 gives a smaller log loss than Model 2 resulting in increased average accuracy throughout training and testing the network.

High-level questions

  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which model achieves the lowest log loss on the test set?",
    "rationale": "Log loss is a measure of model prediction error; lower is better. Comparing Test Model 1 (0.166) and Test Model 2 (0.194) reveals which model generalizes better.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },

      "row_comparison"

#39 · record_id=386 · paper_id=2003.04109v1
QTIP: Quick simulation-based adaptation of Traffic model per Incident Parameters
Partially covered: 1Missing: 3raw 4verified 4source base

Caption

TABLE IV: Performance of Mordinary under normal vs. incident conditions

Gold Description

we measure how worse Mordinary performs on incident conditions, for which it was not trained. The results are summarized in Table IV, and we see that Mordinary deteriorates in comparison with its performance under normal conditions. The deterioration occurs whether or not Mordinary has access to information about uplink and downlink D.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which condition (Normal w/o, Incident with, Incident w/o) achieves the lowest MSD value?",
    "rationale": "MSD measures bias; lower is better. Identifying the condition with minimal bias helps in understanding which scenario the model handles best.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "How does the MAE performance degrade from normal to incident conditions (both with and without U and D)?",
    "rationale": "Comparing MAE between normal and incident conditions quantifies the impact of incidents on average error magnitude.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "Under incident conditions, does including U and D (with) consistently yield higher error metrics compared to without?",
    "rationale": "Comparing incident with vs incident w/o across MSD, MAE, and RMSE reveals whether the inclusion of U and D consistently worsens performance.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "Which condition results in the highest RMSE value?",
    "rationale": "RMSE penalizes large errors; higher RMSE indicates worse overall performance. Identifying the worst condition helps prioritize improvement areas.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  }
]

#40 · record_id=394 · paper_id=1903.07424v1
Communication-Efficient Federated Deep Learning with Asynchronous Model Update and Temporally Weighted Aggregation
Missing: 4raw 4verified 4source base

Caption

TABLE III: Parameter settings for the LSTM.

Gold Description

The architecture of the LSTM used in this study has two 5 × 5 LSTM layers (the first one with cell size = 20 and time steps = 128 and the other with cell size = 10 and the same time steps), a fully connected layer with 128 units and the ReLu activation, and a softmax output layer. The corresponding parameters of the LSTM is given in Table III.

#41 · record_id=427 · paper_id=1803.01378v1
Localization under Topological Uncertainty for Lane Identification of Autonomous Vehicles
Missing: 4raw 4verified 4source base

Caption

TABLE I: Location estimation results

Gold Description

Localization accuracy was tested on 6 hand-annotated datasets gathered by an AV on public, multi-lane roads near Nissan Research Center in Silicon Valley. Each dataset was recorded over about a mile of stop-and-go traffic and ranged in time from 2 to 6 minutes. All road segments had between 3 and 6 lanes, corresponding to between 5 and 11 states. In these experiments no metric location information, such as GPS, was used, and topological ground truth was provided. Because of the intermittent nature of the lane line and vehicle detections, not all timesteps possess enough observations to disambiguate lane-states. Thus, in the results presented in Table we do not consider instances in which either no observations were recorded or the observations voted for at least half of all states, such as seeing only a single lane line immediately to the left of the vehicle. These instances are labeled "Missing Observations". Given sequences of timesteps with little or no observations, it is possible to have multiple states tie for the same belief. Localization is considered correct if the true state is among those with maximum belief, and incorrect otherwise. Length and observation quality are shown so as to give an idea about the difficulty of the dataset. Predictions are made at 100Hz.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which dataset achieves the highest accuracy, and what are its length and missing observations?",

      "row_extrema"

Domain Knowledge Aided Explainable Artificial Intelligence for Intrusion Detection and Response

Caption

Table 6: Performance of unseen attack detection using RF

Gold Description

As shown in Table 6, we see that except for the constructed feature settings (denoted by Cons.), the performances of other feature settings (all, selected, and domain) are similar.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which attack achieves the highest All(%) and what is its count?",
    "rationale": "The All(%) column shows perfect 100% for Infiltration and Heartbleed, both with count=1. This question explores the relationship between high detection rate and limited sample size.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "Which attack has the lowest Cons(%) and what are its other performance percentages?",
    "rationale": "Infiltration and Web Attack-Sql both show 0% Cons(%), but Infiltration has 100% in other metrics while Web Attack-Sql has 0% in All and Sel. This contrast highlights performance inconsistency.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "How does the consistency (Cons.%) vary with the sample size (Count) across attacks?",
    "rationale": "Attacks with high count (e.g., Dos Hulk, Ddos, PortScan) show Cons(%) up to 96.19%, while low-count attacks (e.g., Infiltration, Heartbleed) have extreme Cons(%) values (0% or 100%). This suggests a potential correlation.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_correlation"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "Among attacks with Count > 100, which has the highest Dom(%) and which has the lowest?",
    "rationale": "Filtering by Count > 100 gives robust attacks (e.g., Dos Hulk, Ddos, PortScan). The highest Dom(%) among them is 99.94% (PortScan) and the lowest may indicate a performance gap.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "threshold_count",
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  }
]

#43 · record_id=447 · paper_id=1909.10400v1
Robot Navigation in Crowds by Graph Convolutional Networks with Attention Learned from Human Gaze
Partially covered: 2Missing: 2raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

TABLE III: Additional ablation study to show the advantage of the attention weights trained based on human gaze data.

Gold Description

As shown in Table III, the success rate of G-GCNRL was 10.5% higher than that of D-GCNRL and 5.0% higher than that of U-GCNRL.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which method achieves the highest average success rate, and how does it compare to the other methods?",
    "rationale": "The average success rate column directly indicates overall performance. Identifying the best method highlights the benefit of gaze-trained attention.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema",
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "In which dataset does G-GCNRL show the largest improvement in success rate compared to the worst performing method?",
    "rationale": "This reveals the dataset where gaze attention has the most impact, guiding understanding of its strengths.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison",
      "column_extrema",
      "threshold_count"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "For navigation time, which method has the lowest average time, and is there a trade-off with success rate?",
    "rationale": "Navigation time and success rate may be inversely related; exploring both metrics provides insight into efficiency vs. effectiveness.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison",
      "column_correlation"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "How does the success rate of attention-based methods (G-GCNRL) compare to non-attention methods across all datasets?",
    "rationale": "Comparing average and per-dataset success rates directly addresses the advantage of gaze-trained attention weights.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison",
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  }
]

#44 · record_id=461 · paper_id=1907.02394v2
Automating Distributed Tiered Storage Management in Cluster Computing
Missing: 5raw 5verified 5source retry

Caption

Table 3: Job size distributions. The jobs are binned by their data sizes in our FB and CMU workloads

Gold Description

Table 3 shows the distribution of jobs by count, cluster resources they consume, amount of I/O they generate, and aggregate task execution time. The jobs in both workloads exhibit a heavy-tailed distribution of input sizes,

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which bin has the highest percentage of jobs for FB and for CMU, and what are those percentages?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the bin with the largest fraction of jobs in each workload reveals the most common job size, which is critical for workload characterization.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "Which bin consumes the highest percentage of resources for FB and for CMU?",
    "rationale": "Finding which bin uses the most resources highlights where system resources are concentrated, informing tiered storage design.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "How do the task times compare between FB and CMU for each bin? Is the pattern consistent across bins?",
    "rationale": "Comparing task times across workloads for each size bin reveals differences in job duration, which may indicate different I/O or processing patterns.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "As data size increases from A to F, what is the trend in the percentage of I/O for FB and for CMU?",
    "rationale": "Analyzing the trend of I/O percentage with data size helps understand if larger jobs are more I/O-intensive, affecting storage tier placement.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "trend"
    ]
  }
]

#45 · record_id=468 · paper_id=1909.02966v1
Robust Barrier Functions for a Fully Autonomous, Remotely Accessible Swarm-Robotics Testbed
Covered: 1raw 1verified 1source retry

Caption

Table II: Comparison of the Wall-Clock Times (WCTs) for solving the Quadratic Programs with and without the robust CBF formulation. The last entry is the duration during which the constraint was violated for each experiment.

Gold Description

Focusing on the run time, solving the robust CBF QP averaged a wall-clock time of 4.5 ms, translating to a frequency of approximately 220 Hz, resulting in only a 25 Hz decrease compared to solving the non-robust CBF QP. The reason the frequency decreases slightly is that the only computation added is a min operation over p values, which is linear with respect to p (O(p)), where p is the number of points forming the convex hull of the disturbance. The comparison of the wall-clock times of both experiments is shown in Table II. The robust CBF formulation (left) encounters 0 collisions whereas the non-robust CBF formulation (right) periodically encounters collisions as shown in Table II.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "How does the average wall-clock time of Robust CBF compare to Non-Robust CBF?",
    "rationale": "Identifying which quadratic programming formulation yields lower average computation time is crucial for real-time control applications.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "Which formulation exhibits lower variance in wall-clock times?",
    "rationale": "Lower variance indicates more predictable computation times, which is important for system timing guarantees.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "How does the average frequency differ between the robust and non-robust CBF formulations?",

    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "Does the robust CBF formulation eliminate constraint violations?",
    "rationale": "The primary motivation for robust CBF is to guarantee constraint satisfaction; comparing the time violated quantifies its effectiveness.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "threshold_count"
    ]
  }
]

#46 · record_id=477 · paper_id=2001.09018v1
Are Distributed Ledger Technologies Ready for Smart Transportation Systems?1footnote 11footnote 1 Working draft paper, submitted for evaluation to an international conference.
Partially covered: 3Missing: 1raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

Table I: Results on IOTA, with 60, 120, 240 buses.

Gold Description

The amount of errors is quite high, as well as the measured latencies. Thus, these tests seem to conclude that, at the time of writing, the IOTA DLT is not fully structured to support smart services for transportation systems. On the other hand, the good news is that if we carefully select the full node to issue a transaction, the performances definitely improve. In fact, our third scheme "Adaptive RTT" has a low amount of errors, on average around 0.8%. Measured latencies are lower than other approaches. Still, the average latency amounts to 23 seconds, which is far from a real-time update of the DLT. charts further confirm the better performance obtained by the "Adaptive RTT" scheme. These Results confirm that "Adaptive RTT" provides better results. Average latencies are definitely lower than other schemes. In all cases, average latencies increase with the number of buses. To better emphasize the outcomes, Table reports some summarized statistics (shown in the box plots) and the error rates. Actually, the main difference on the performance of the approaches is on the amount of errors. While the average error for "Adaptive RTT" is ∼ 1%, for the other two schemes we have errors well above 15%. These error rates are clearly unacceptable, meaning that these approaches are unusable.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "How does the average latency change as the number of buses increases from 60 to 240?",
    "rationale": "Understanding the scalability of IOTA with respect to bus count is crucial for smart transportation systems, as latency directly impacts real-time decision-making.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "trend"
    ]
  },

    "id": "h2",
    "question": "Which configuration achieves the lowest error rate across all bus counts?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the configuration with minimal errors helps in assessing the reliability of IOTA for critical transportation applications.",

      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",

      "column_correlation"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "For the 240-bus scenario, which trial (first, second, or third) yields the best combination of low latency and low errors?",
    "rationale": "Comparing runs within the same bus count helps identify the most efficient configuration for high-demand scenarios in smart transportation.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  }
]

#47 · record_id=492 · paper_id=1909.13599v2
End-to-End Motion Planning of Quadrotors Using Deep Reinforcement Learning
Missing: 4raw 4verified 4source retry

Caption

TABLE I: Evaluative test results in AirSim.

Gold Description

Table states the values for reward, navigation distance, navigation time, and crash rate. In the first three environments, the agent is able to learn a policy which is close to the optimal. Successful navigation from the start in these relatively simple environments. The navigation time performance is also desirable as being close to 60s in 1m/s is observed for each trial to the goal setting. The crash rate is 0% in these environments, even for the narrow corridor in Env. 3. Still, the agent successfully reaches the goal position in 67% of the trials. In terms of safety, the agent completes 73% of the trials without a crash in these dense environments. On the other hand, there are some trials such as the third one in Env. 4 in which the agent cannot reach the goal position but does not take decisions which might yield a crash either. In the last three environments, the agent yields better performance as compared to the former group. It successfully reaches the goal position in 87% of the trials. The other 13% stands for two trials in Env. 8. The agent yields fairly safe flights with collision-free flight percentage of 86% over the trials in AirSim. It reaches the goal point successfully during 88% of these safe flights in AirSim

  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which environment and run achieved the highest total reward, and what are its navigation distance and time?",

#48 · record_id=517 · paper_id=1809.08559v1
Which Source Code Plagiarism Detection Approach is More Humane?
Missing: 3raw 3verified 3source retry

Caption

TABLE II: Considered aspects while suspecting plagiarism cases

Gold Description

Table II displays those aspects, including their occurrences (calculated based on the number of respondents mentioning it) and their relationship to evaluated plagiarism detection approaches. Most of mentioned aspects are related to SBA. Hence, it can be stated that SBA is more effective than ABA from think-aloud evaluation perspective.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which aspect has the highest number of occurrences, and what is that count?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the most commonly considered aspect helps understand the primary focus in plagiarism detection based on this table.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "Which aspect has the lowest number of occurrences?",
    "rationale": "Determining the least considered aspect can indicate which factors are less prioritized in plagim cases.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "column_extrema"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "How does the occurrence count of 'Semantic' compare to the combined occurrence counts of the other four aspects?",
    "rationale": "This comparison reveals whether a single aspect dominates or if consideration is spread across multiple aspects.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "Are there any aspects with exactly the same occurrence count?",
    "rationale": "Identifying ties can highlight aspects that are considered equally important in the dataset.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison",
      "threshold_count"
    ]
  }
]

#49 · record_id=535 · paper_id=1907.03390v2
Augmenting Knowledge through Statistical,Goal-oriented Human-Robot Dialog
Covered: 1Missing: 1Contradicted: 1raw 3verified 3source retry

Caption

TABLE II: Results of the human participant experiment.

Gold Description

By the end of each dialog, each participant filled out a survey form that includes prompts: Q1, Task is easy to participants; Q2, Robot understood participant; Q3, Robot frustrated participant; Q4, Participant will use the robot in the future. The response choices range from 0 (Strongly disagree) to 4 (Strongly agree). Table II shows the average scores. At the 0.1 confidence level, our dialog agent performed significantly better in response for Q3 (frustrated) and Q4 (usefulness). There is no significance difference observed in responses to the other two questions.

High-level questions
[
  {
    "id": "h1",
    "question": "Which agent has the highest overall average score across all four questions?",
    "rationale": "Identifying the overall better-performing agent summarizes the main experimental result.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h2",
    "question": "On which question does the dialog agent outperform the baseline agent by the largest margin?",
    "rationale": "Finding the largest performance gap highlights the dialog agent's strongest relative advantage.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h3",
    "question": "For which question does the baseline agent perform closest to or better than the dialog agent?",
    "rationale": "Identifying where the baseline competes best helps understand its relative strengths.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "row_comparison"
    ]
  },
  {
    "id": "h4",
    "question": "Is there a trend in the scores across questions (e.g., decreasing, increasing) for either agent?",
    "rationale": "Detecting trends can reveal whether performance changes with question order or difficulty.",
    "suggested_operations": [
      "trend"
    ]
  }
]

#50 · record_id=545 · paper_id=1903.12359v2
Parallelizable global conformal parameterization of simply-connected surfaces via partial welding
Covered: 1Partially covered: 1Missing: 1raw 3verified 3source retry

Caption

Table 6: The performance of our proposed PGCP method for parameterizing the bunny model, with different number of subdomains used. Note that the welding algorithm requires at least two subdomains and hence we use n=2 as the baseline. For this reason, we define the parallel speedup to be Sn=T2Tn where Ti is the time taken with i subdomains used, and the parallel efficiency to be En=Snn/2.

Gold Description

Table 6 shows the experimental results with 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 subdomains. Note that 2 is used as the baseline as the welding algorithm requires at least two subdomains. From the experimental result, we observe a speedup achieved by exploiting parallelization. To evaluate the parallel efficiency, we consider the ratio En = Sn n/2 , where Sn is the speedup achieved by n subdomains compared to the baseline and n/2 is the subdomain ratio. It can be observed that En is close to 1 for small n but shows a decreasing trend as n increases.
